full_context: Momentum form of Newton’s second law: F = dp/dt. The net force equals the time rate of change of linear momentum. For constant mass and time-varying velocity, it reduces to F = ma. Valid for particles and rigid bodies in classical mechanics at low speeds.

variable_definitions.name: Force
variable_definitions.meaning: Time rate of change of momentum, cause of motion change

variable_definitions.name: Momentum
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of mass and velocity, measure of motion

potential_questions: An object of mass m=2 kg has momentum p(t) = 3t^2 + 2t (kg·m/s). Find the force F at t=2 s.
task_description: 1. Given momentum function p(t). 2. Compute derivative dp/dt. 3. Obtain force function F(t). 4. Substitute t=2 s to evaluate F(t).

full_context: Definition of linear momentum: p = m v. In classical low-speed mechanics, momentum is proportional to mass and velocity; vectorial in multiple dimensions.

variable_definitions.name: Momentum
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of mass and velocity, measure of motion

variable_definitions.name: Mass
variable_definitions.meaning: Quantity of matter, measure of inertia

variable_definitions.name: Velocity
variable_definitions.meaning: Time rate of displacement, speed and direction

potential_questions: A car of mass m=1200 kg moves at v=20 m/s. Find its momentum p.
task_description: 1. Identify mass m. 2. Identify velocity v. 3. Compute p = m v. 4. Output p with unit.

full_context: Translational kinetic energy: E_k = (1/2) m v^2. Valid in classical low-speed mechanics, energy due to translational motion.

variable_definitions.name: Kinetic energy
variable_definitions.meaning: Energy possessed by a moving body

potential_questions: A 5 kg object moves at v=10 m/s. Find its kinetic energy E_k.
task_description: 1. Identify mass m. 2. Identify velocity v. 3. Compute v^2. 4. Compute (1/2) m v^2. 5. Output E_k.

full_context: Angular momentum of a rigid body about a fixed axis: L = I ω. I is mass moment of inertia, ω is angular velocity. Valid for rigid-body rotation.

variable_definitions.name: Angular momentum
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of inertia and angular velocity, measure of rotational motion

potential_questions: A flywheel with I=0.5 kg·m² rotates at ω=20 rad/s. Find angular momentum L.
task_description: 1. Identify moment of inertia I. 2. Identify angular velocity ω. 3. Compute L = I ω. 4. Output L.

full_context: Rotational kinetic energy of a rigid body: E_r = (1/2) I ω². Valid for fixed-axis rotation with uniform angular velocity.
potential_questions: A rotor with I=2 kg·m² rotates at ω=5 rad/s. Find rotational kinetic energy E_r.
task_description: 1. Identify I. 2. Identify ω. 3. Compute ω². 4. Compute E_r = (1/2) I ω². 5. Output E_r.

full_context: Bernoulli equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid ideal flow along a streamline: sum of static pressure, dynamic pressure, and potential head is constant.
potential_questions: At a pipe section: P₁=200 kPa, v₁=2 m/s, h₁=5 m. Downstream: h₂=2 m, v₂=4 m/s. ρ=1000 kg/m³, g=9.8 m/s². Find P₂.
task_description: 1. Write upstream term: P₁ + ½ρv₁² + ρgh₁. 2. Write downstream term: P₂ + ½ρv₂² + ρgh₂. 3. Equate and solve for P₂. 4. Check unit consistency.

full_context: Volume flow rate: Q = A v. For a round tube, A = π d²/4. Valid for steady incompressible flow.

variable_definitions.name: Volume flow rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Volume per unit time passing a section

potential_questions: Pipe diameter d=50 mm, flow speed v=1.5 m/s. Find volume flow rate Q.
task_description: 1. Compute A = π d²/4. 2. Identify v. 3. Compute Q = A v. 4. Output Q.

full_context: Reynolds number Re = (ρ v D)/μ, ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Used to classify flow: laminar for Re < 2300, turbulent for Re > 4000.

variable_definitions.name: Reynolds number
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of inertia to viscosity, flow regime indicator

potential_questions: Pipe D=0.1 m, v=0.5 m/s, ρ=1000 kg/m³, μ=1.0×10⁻³ Pa·s. Find Re and classify flow.
task_description: 1. Identify ρ, v, D, μ. 2. Compute ρ v D. 3. Compute Re = (ρ v D)/μ. 4. Classify using threshold values.

full_context: Fourier’s law of 1D steady heat conduction: q = -k dT/dx. Negative sign indicates heat flows from high to low temperature.
potential_questions: Plate thickness L=0.02 m, T₁=100°C, T₂=20°C, k=50 W/(m·K). Find heat flux q.
task_description: 1. Identify k. 2. Approximate dT/dx ≈ (T₂−T₁)/L. 3. Compute q = −k dT/dx. 4. Output q.

full_context: Thermal efficiency: η = W / Q_h. Ratio of net work output to heat input from high-temperature source.

variable_definitions.name: Thermal efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of useful work to heat input

variable_definitions.name: Net work output
variable_definitions.meaning: Useful work delivered by heat engine

potential_questions: Heat engine absorbs Q_h=1000 J, outputs W=300 J. Find efficiency η.
task_description: 1. Identify W. 2. Identify Q_h. 3. Compute η = W/Q_h. 4. Output η (may convert to percentage).

full_context: Gear ratio by tooth count: i = N₂/N₁. Ideal no-slip speed ratio: n₁/n₂ = N₂/N₁.

variable_definitions.name: Gear ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of driven teeth to driving teeth, speed ratio

variable_definitions.name: Driver teeth
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of teeth on input gear

variable_definitions.name: Driven teeth
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of teeth on output gear

potential_questions: Driver teeth N₁=20, driven teeth N₂=60. Find gear ratio i.
task_description: 1. Identify N₂. 2. Identify N₁. 3. Compute i = N₂/N₁. 4. Output i.

full_context: Linear spring stiffness: k = F/δ. Force proportional to deflection within Hooke’s law range.

variable_definitions.name: Spring stiffness
variable_definitions.meaning: Force per unit deflection, measure of rigidity

potential_questions: Spring force F=100 N causes deflection δ=0.02 m. Find stiffness k.
task_description: 1. Identify F. 2. Identify δ. 3. Compute k = F/δ. 4. Output k.

full_context: Rolling bearing basic rating life (in millions of revolutions): L₁₀ = (C/P)^p. Exponent p=3 for ball bearings, p=10/3 for roller bearings.
potential_questions: Ball bearing C=25 kN, P=5 kN. Find L₁₀ in millions of revolutions.
task_description: 1. Identify C and P. 2. Select exponent p. 3. Compute ratio C/P. 4. Compute L₁₀ = (C/P)^p. 5. Output L₁₀.

full_context: Natural angular frequency of undamped single-degree-of-freedom system: ω_c = √(k/m). Derived from m ẍ + k x = 0.
potential_questions: Mass m=10 kg, stiffness k=400 N/m. Find natural angular frequency ω_c.
task_description: 1. Identify k. 2. Identify m. 3. Compute k/m. 4. Take square root for ω_c. 5. Output ω_c.

full_context: Natural frequency of undamped SDOF system: f = ω/(2π) = (1/(2π))√(k/m).
potential_questions: System k=100 N/m, m=1 kg. Find natural frequency f.
task_description: 1. Identify k and m. 2. Compute √(k/m). 3. Compute f = (1/(2π))√(k/m). 4. Output f.

full_context: Damping ratio: ζ = c/(2√(m k)). Ratio of actual damping to critical damping.

variable_definitions.name: Damping ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual to critical damping

potential_questions: System m=5 kg, k=500 N/m, c=50 N·s/m. Find damping ratio ζ.
task_description: 1. Compute √(m k). 2. Compute 2√(m k). 3. Compute ζ = c/(2√(m k)). 4. Output ζ.

full_context: Theoretical stress concentration factor: K_t = σ_max / σ_nom. Measures local stress rise due to geometric discontinuities.

variable_definitions.name: Theoretical stress concentration factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of local max stress to nominal stress

variable_definitions.name: Maximum local stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Peak stress at notch or hole

variable_definitions.name: Nominal stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Stress from elementary mechanics on net section

potential_questions: Net section stress σ_nom=80 MPa, hole-edge stress σ_max=200 MPa. Find K_t.
task_description: 1. Identify σ_max. 2. Identify σ_nom. 3. Compute K_t = σ_max/σ_nom. 4. Output K_t.

full_context: Goodman linear mean stress correction: adjusted fatigue limit accounting for static mean stress.

variable_definitions.name: Corrected fatigue limit
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable stress amplitude with mean stress

potential_questions: Material σ_u=600 MPa, σ_a=150 MPa, σ_m=100 MPa. Estimate S_f using Goodman.
task_description: 1. Identify σ_u, σ_a, σ_m. 2. Compute ratio σ_m/σ_u. 3. Compute 1 − σ_m/σ_u. 4. Compute S_f = σ_a / (1 − σ_m/σ_u). 5. Output S_f.

full_context: General Euler–Bernoulli beam equation (variable section or material). For constant EI: E I d⁴w/dx⁴ = q.

variable_definitions.name: Area moment of inertia
variable_definitions.meaning: Moment of inertia about neutral axis

potential_questions: Simply supported beam L=4 m, E=200 GPa, I=8×10⁻⁶ m⁴, uniform load q=10 kN/m. Write governing equation.
task_description: 1. Identify E, I, q. 2. Use constant EI for uniform section. 3. Write E I d⁴w/dx⁴ = q. 4. State boundary conditions (simply supported: w=0, M=0 at ends).

full_context: Coulomb friction model: friction force F_f proportional to normal force F_n, with coefficient μ. Minimum sliding force ≈ maximum static friction.

variable_definitions.name: Coefficient of friction
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of friction to normal force

potential_questions: Mass m=10 kg, μ=0.3, g=9.8 m/s². Find minimum force to start sliding.
task_description: 1. Compute normal force F_n = m g. 2. Compute max friction F_f = μ F_n. 3. Minimum force F_min ≈ F_f. 4. Output F_min.
